questions.question: The Titanic sank in which year?
questions.explanation: The RMS Titanic sank on April 15, 1912, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic Ocean on its maiden voyage from Southampton to New York. About 1,500 of the 2,224 passengers and crew died. The disaster led to major changes in maritime regulations, including requiring ships to carry enough lifeboats for everyone aboard.
questions.memory_hook: Titanic sank in 1912. Maiden voyage. 1,500 deaths. The ship was said to be unsinkable by the press — the White Star Line itself never made that claim. Four days into its first trip.

questions.question: Which physicist formulated the theory of special relativity in 1905 and derived the famous equation E=mc², showing mass and energy are interchangeable?
questions.explanation: Albert Einstein published four groundbreaking papers in 1905 — his 'miracle year' — including the theory of special relativity and the mass-energy equivalence equation E=mc². He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921, not for relativity, but for his explanation of the photoelectric effect.
questions.memory_hook: Einstein 1905: E=mc², special relativity. Nobel 1921 for photoelectric effect.

questions.question: The atomic bombs dropped on Japan in August 1945 ended World War II. On which two cities were they dropped?
questions.explanation: The United States dropped an atomic bomb on Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, and another on Nagasaki on August 9, 1945. Hiroshima's bomb ('Little Boy') killed approximately 70,000-80,000 immediately; Nagasaki's ('Fat Man') killed about 40,000 immediately. Japan's Emperor Hirohito announced surrender on August 15, ending World War II. The bombings remain the only use of nuclear weapons in warfare.
questions.memory_hook: Hiroshima (Aug 6) then Nagasaki (Aug 9), then Japan surrendered. Three days between the bombs. The only nuclear weapons ever used in war. The decision is still debated by historians today.

questions.question: The construction of the Great Wall of China was started during which dynasty to protect against northern invaders?
questions.explanation: The first sections of the Great Wall were built during the Qin Dynasty (221-206 BC) under Emperor Qin Shi Huang, who unified China and ordered the connection of existing walls to form a northern frontier defense. Most of the wall visible today was built during the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644). The wall stretches over 20,000 km and took hundreds of years and millions of workers to build.
questions.memory_hook: Great Wall started in the Qin Dynasty. Most of what tourists see today is Ming Dynasty. It took over 1,000 years of building across multiple dynasties — no single emperor built the whole thing.

questions.question: Which swimmer holds the record for the most Olympic medals of all time?
questions.explanation: Michael Phelps won 28 Olympic medals (23 gold, 3 silver, 2 bronze) across four Olympic Games from 2000 to 2016, making him the most decorated Olympian in history. He won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics alone, beating the single-Games record. He competed in multiple individual and relay events simultaneously.
questions.memory_hook: Phelps = 28 Olympic medals, 23 gold. No one is close. At the 2008 Beijing Olympics he won 8 golds in one Games. His arm span (193 cm) exceeds his height, giving him a physical advantage.

questions.question: In cricket, what is the maximum number of balls in an 'over'?
questions.explanation: An over in cricket consists of 6 legal deliveries bowled by the same bowler from one end of the pitch. After each over, a different bowler delivers from the other end. If a bowler delivers a wide or no-ball, an additional delivery is required. The number of overs in a match varies: Test matches have unlimited overs across 5 days, while T20 matches have just 20 overs per side.
questions.memory_hook: Cricket over = 6 balls. One bowler, one end, 6 deliveries. Then switch ends, different bowler. In T20 cricket, each team gets just 20 overs (120 balls) total — which is why it only takes 3 hours.

questions.question: What color is the distinctive jersey awarded each day to the leader of the Tour de France cycling race?
questions.explanation: The yellow jersey (maillot jaune) has been awarded to the Tour de France leader since 1919. It was introduced by race organizer Henri Desgrange, reportedly because L'Auto, the newspaper sponsoring the race, was printed on yellow paper. A green jersey goes to the points leader; white to the best young rider.
questions.memory_hook: Tour de France leader = yellow jersey (maillot jaune). Since 1919.

questions.question: 'Lose Yourself' by Eminem was the first rap song to win the Academy Award for Best Original Song. From which film?
questions.explanation: 'Lose Yourself' won the Academy Award for Best Original Song in 2003, taken from Eminem's semi-autobiographical film 8 Mile (2002). Eminem did not attend the ceremony and was reportedly asleep when the award was announced. The song topped charts in 11 countries and is considered one of the greatest rap songs ever written.
questions.memory_hook: 'Lose Yourself' = first rap song to win an Oscar. Eminem slept through the ceremony. From 8 Mile — a movie about Eminem's own story of coming up in Detroit.

questions.question: Which 1977 George Lucas film launched one of the most successful film franchises in history?
questions.explanation: Star Wars (later subtitled A New Hope) was released on May 25, 1977, and became a cultural phenomenon. It was groundbreaking for its special effects, sound design, and mythological storytelling. It spawned 11 sequel and prequel films, animated series, theme parks, and merchandise that has generated over $70 billion in revenue — the highest-grossing franchise in history at the time.
questions.memory_hook: Star Wars, 1977. George Lucas. Studios rejected it repeatedly before Fox took a chance. Lucas gave up his director's fee in exchange for merchandising rights — that decision made him a billionaire.

questions.question: Which musician is known as the 'King of Rock and Roll'?
questions.explanation: Elvis Presley earned the title 'King of Rock and Roll' through his pioneering fusion of country, blues, and gospel music in the 1950s. He recorded hits including 'Hound Dog,' 'Jailhouse Rock,' and 'Love Me Tender,' and starred in 33 films. His hip-swiveling performance style was considered scandalous on television in the 1950s. He died in 1977 at age 42.
questions.memory_hook: Elvis = King of Rock and Roll. His TV appearances were so scandalous they filmed him from the waist up. He took Black musical traditions and brought them to white mainstream America — which made him famous and raises ongoing questions about credit and appropriation.

questions.question: The Rhine River flows through Germany and empties into which sea?
questions.explanation: The Rhine originates in the Swiss Alps and flows approximately 1,230 km through Switzerland, Austria, Liechtenstein, Germany, France, and the Netherlands before emptying into the North Sea. It passes through several major European cities including Basel, Cologne, and Rotterdam. The Rhine has been a central artery of European trade and culture for millennia.
questions.memory_hook: Rhine flows to the North Sea. Starts in the Swiss Alps, ends in the Netherlands. Major European trade route for 2,000 years — Romans, medieval merchants, and modern shipping all used it.

questions.question: What is the term for an animal that eats both plants and meat?
questions.explanation: Omnivores eat both plant and animal matter. Humans are omnivores. So are bears, pigs, raccoons, and crows. The flexibility to eat a wide range of foods is a major evolutionary advantage — omnivores can adapt to varied environments and food availability. In contrast, strict carnivores (like obligate cats) and herbivores can struggle when their preferred food sources are scarce.
questions.memory_hook: Omnivore = eats everything. Herbivore = plants only. Carnivore = meat only. Humans are omnivores — we evolved to eat whatever was available. It's why we can live everywhere from the Arctic to the tropics.
